content.plain_english: This week your child is collecting and showing information using tally charts, pictograms and block diagrams. They are reading these to answer questions like 'which is the most popular' and 'how many altogether'.
content.in_school: Children gather their own data — favourite colour, type of pet, lunchtime choice — using tally marks, with the fifth mark crossing the previous four. They turn the tallies into pictograms (with one picture standing for one, two, five or ten items) and into block diagrams.
content.home_activity: Over the weekend, ask each family member their favourite biscuit, fruit or breakfast. Help your child make a tally chart on a piece of paper as you go round. At the end, ask: which had the most? Which had the fewest? How many people did we ask in total? Five minutes including the asking.
dinner_table_questions: If the pictogram shows three smiley faces and each face stands for two votes, how many people voted?
What is the most popular pudding in our family — how could we find out?
Why do we use tally marks in groups of five?

key_vocabulary.definition: A way of counting things by drawing lines, with every fifth line crossing the previous four to make a group of 5.
A chart that uses pictures to show how many. A key tells you what each picture stands for.
A chart where each block stands for one thing, stacked into columns to show totals.
Information that has been collected, often by counting or asking questions.
A small note on a chart that tells you what the pictures or blocks stand for.

example_questions: On a tally chart, how do you show 7?
A pictogram uses one apple to mean 2 children. How many children does 4 apples stand for?
Look at this block diagram: red has 5 blocks, blue has 8, green has 3. How many children chose red or blue?
content.times_table_tip: Pictograms in Year 2 often use one symbol to stand for 2, 5 or 10 items. Knowing the 2, 5 and 10 times tables means your child can read those scales straight away.
